fact: May 2 is exactly 122 days into the year, which means there are exactly 243 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, dandelions are typically going wild right now, turning entire fields into bright yellow carpets that look like someone spilled sunshine everywhere.
Around May 2 in many temperate regions, soil temperatures often reach about 50°F, which is when mosquito eggs that have been dormant all winter start to hatch.
The sun is climbing so fast in the sky that people in northern cities like Seattle typically gain about 3 minutes of daylight every single day around May 2.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, May 2 usually marks the time when leaves start their color-changing show before falling off trees for winter.
May 2 always falls under the zodiac sign of Taurus, which is represented by the bull.
In many ponds around this date, tadpoles that hatched in early spring are often starting to sprout their back legs, looking like tiny aliens with tails.
May 2 sits almost perfectly between the spring equinox and summer solstice, making it like the 'middle child' of spring in the Northern Hemisphere.
In many cold northern regions, early May is typically 'mud season' - when snow melts and turns everything into a squishy mess that makes weird squelching sounds when you walk.
In temperate regions, cherry blossom petals are often raining down like pink snow around this time, creating thick carpets that muffle footsteps.
When there's a new moon around May 2, the dark night sky becomes perfect for spotting shooting stars from the Eta Aquariids meteor shower, which is typically active in early May.
In northern maple forests, early May is when trees start pumping out so much sap that you can sometimes actually hear it dripping on a quiet day.
